main_summary.summary: Freddie Greene Biddle, born February 15, 1945 in Greenwood, Mississippi, recounts her trajectory from a Black landowning family in the Mississippi Delta into SNCC organizing work between 1962 and 1968. Her father, a self-employed house painter and NAACP member, and her schoolteacher mother registered to vote in the late 1950s. After her brother Dwight applied to Ole Miss as the second Black student, the family came under sustained pressure. Greene began attending SNCC mass meetings in Greenwood in 1962 alongside Sam Block, Willie Peacock, Robert Parris Moses, Medgar Evers, and Amzie Moore. She helped distribute the food airlifted by Dick Gregory and trucked in by Ivanhoe Donaldson after Leflore County cut off federal commodities. In 1963 a shotgun blast hit the family home after her brother George attended a SNCC meeting. She enrolled at Dillard University in New Orleans, connected with Tulane organizers through the Newman Center, and joined the 1964 McComb summer project, where the freedom house was bombed. She attended the Atlantic City Democratic Convention with the MFDP delegation, worked the Jackson fairground mass arrests of summer 1965, and joined the Atlanta SNCC office under James Forman and Ruby Doris Smith Robinson on the switchboard and in finance. She left SNCC in June 1968.
key_themes: Greenwood and Leflore County movement infrastructure
Mississippi Freedom Summer and McComb project
MFDP and the Atlantic City convention
Black landownership and family economic insulation
main_summary.historical_significance:
Greene's testimony documents the SNCC Greenwood project from the perspective of a teenager whose family hosted organizers including Stokely Carmichael, with first-person detail on the 1962 Leflore County food cutoff, the Dick Gregory airlift, the shotgun attack on her family's home in March 1963, and the bombing of the McComb freedom house during the 1964 summer project. Her account links the Mississippi Delta organizing base to the Atlanta SNCC office under Forman and Ruby Doris Smith Robinson, including the internal staff shift around the 1966 Meredith March Against Fear and Black Power.

The interview also preserves granular biographical material on Greenwood's spatial segregation, the social-security and house-painting economy that sustained activist Black families, the Heffners' departure under white community pressure after meeting with a SNCC-affiliated minister and Dennis Sweeney, and the Jackson fairground mass-arrest detention conditions of summer 1965, a comparatively under-documented episode in the run-up to the Voting Rights Act.

chapters.title: Greenwood Family Roots
chapters.summary: Greene introduces her 1945 birth in Greenwood, Mississippi, her mother's upbringing in the same Howard Street house owned by her great-grandmother, and her father's farming background. She describes her father's house-painting business, her mother's training as a teacher at Tougaloo, and the social-security planning that would later support her father when movement involvement cost him work.
related_events: Black landownership in the Mississippi Delta

chapters.title: Segregated Greenwood
chapters.summary: Greene describes growing up in a fully segregated Greenwood, segregated schools using hand-me-down textbooks, no integration until 1970, and the spatial separation between the Black community on Howard Street, the antebellum white North Greenwood, the Jewish community, and Chinese-owned grocery stores. She recounts her father's protective rules after Emmett Till's body passed through a Greenwood funeral home.
related_events: Brown v. Board (post-1954 Mississippi segregation maintenance)

chapters.title: Father's Protective Discipline
chapters.summary: Greene describes how her father's caution about white-controlled bootlegging networks and the informant economy shaped the family's safety practices. She contrasts her parents' late-1950s voter registration, done quietly, without actually voting, with the public organizing that would follow.

chapters.title: Entering the Movement
chapters.summary: In 1962 Greene's brother Dwight, returning from the Navy, applied to be the second Black student at Ole Miss and was summoned with their father to a meeting with the Greenwood mayor demanding the application be withdrawn. He went to work for the Mississippi Free Press in Jackson, where he met Robert Parris Moses, Sam Block, Willie Peacock, Reggie Robinson, Medgar Evers, and Amzie Moore. Sam Block and Willie Peacock arrived in Greenwood that summer, and Greene began attending SNCC mass meetings as a high school student.

chapters.title: Leflore Food Cutoff and Distribution
chapters.summary: Greene describes the 1962 Leflore County, Mississippi termination of federal surplus commodities as retaliation against the voter registration drive, coinciding with the first season the mechanical cotton picker reduced Delta wages. She helped distribute the powdered milk, peanut butter, and grain that Dick Gregory airlifted and Ivanhoe Donaldson trucked in, working out of Wesley Methodist Church two blocks from her home.

chapters.title: SNCC Workers and New Horizons
chapters.summary: Greene recalls the SNCC field staff, Stokely Carmichael speaking of Trinidad and New York, Eleanor Holmes at Yale Law School, Tim Jenkins, opening a window onto a world beyond segregated Greenwood. She names the local supporters: June Johnson's mother, the Silas McGee family farm outside Greenwood, Reverend Aaron Johnson of the renamed First Christian Church, Reverend Sterling Tucker, and her own family. She describes how most Black teachers stayed out of the movement out of fear of losing their jobs.

chapters.title: Shotgun Attack and Going to College
chapters.summary: In March 1963, after her brother George attended a SNCC office meeting in Greenwood, a car followed him home and someone fired a shotgun blast through the front of the family house. Her father interpreted the attack as confirmation that the family had to keep organizing. Greene left for Dillard University in New Orleans in September 1963, traveling by train from Winona, the only way she had ever seen the campus.

chapters.title: Pete Seeger and the McGee Farm
chapters.summary: Greene recalls the SNCC-organized concerts on the McGee family farm outside Greenwood featuring Pete Seeger and the Freedom Singers, and the Free Southern Theater's traveling plays across Mississippi. She frames these as cultural enrichment that black Greenwood schools never offered and that the segregated public library blocked her from accessing.

chapters.title: Dillard and Tulane Connection
chapters.summary: Greene describes the difficulty of her freshman year at Dillard, where no student movement organization existed, while the Birmingham church bombing and Medgar Evers's assassination unfolded back home. She connected with Cathy Cade or Casey Hayden at the Tulane Newman Center, navigating the strict in loco parentis rules that required parental written permission for any car ride off campus.

chapters.title: McComb Summer Project 1964
chapters.summary: Greene joined the 1964 Mississippi Summer Project, traveling to the Oxford, Ohio orientation and then with the Southwest Mississippi contingent to Washington to lobby congressional representatives for federal protection. The Schwerner-Chaney-Goodman disappearance reached the orientation while she was in DC. She went to McComb, Mississippi to work on voter canvassing and Freedom Schools alongside her brother George; eleven volunteers shared a freedom house that staff voted to expand against George's objection, and that night the house was bombed with dynamite at the garage door, injuring Curtis Hayes with glass fragments.

chapters.title: The Heffners and McComb Isolation
chapters.summary: Greene recounts how nine Black churches were burned in the McComb area that summer with no white community protest. She corrects the public version of the Heffner story: an Oregon minister-volunteer who knew Mr. Heffner from military service, paired with Stanford student Dennis Sweeney to pass as father and son, visited the Heffners, leading to the family's social ostracism and eventual departure from McComb. No white voices in McComb spoke out against the bombings.

chapters.title: MFDP and Atlantic City
chapters.summary: Greene canvassed and ran the precinct meetings that selected the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party delegates, then traveled to the Atlantic City Democratic Convention with the delegation. She believed the delegates would seat the MFDP. Her takeaway from the rejection: Mississippi could maintain segregation only because the rest of the country did not care, Fannie Lou Hamer's televised testimony made the national stakes visible.

chapters.title: Jackson Fairground Detention
chapters.summary: Greene returned for a difficult second year at Dillard, then left college after her sophomore year to work full-time on voter registration in Jackson. During the summer 1965 Jackson mass demonstrations, she was arrested and held a week at the Mississippi state fairground, which had been converted into a detention site. She describes the concrete floor sleeping conditions, the tin-tray meal line of syrup and light bread for breakfast and grits for lunch, and the work of older field staff trying to maintain morale among hundreds of arrestees as days stretched on with no bail-out date.

chapters.title: Atlanta SNCC Office
chapters.summary: James Forman recruited Greene to the Atlanta SNCC headquarters, where she rotated from the switchboard into finance work alongside SNCC bookkeeper Chessie Johnson and Ruby Doris Smith Robinson. Greene describes Forman's organizational philosophy of cycling field staff through the office and vice versa to build mutual understanding, and Ruby Doris Smith Robinson's mentorship pushing her to learn finance and skills beyond her Mississippi exposure. Her brother George stayed in the field, working in the Lowndes County, Alabama and Greene County, Alabama projects.

chapters.title: Black Power and SNCC's Funding Collapse
chapters.summary: Greene describes how the 1966 Meredith March Against Fear call for Black Power triggered an immediate and sustained drop in SNCC donations and a hostile media response. She situates the staff decision that white organizers should work in white communities while Black organizers worked in Black communities, characterizing the policy as more nuanced than the press portrayed. She left SNCC in June 1968, two months after King's assassination, as the organization's resources collapsed.

chapters.title: Stokely Carmichael as Family
chapters.summary: Greene describes Stokely Carmichael as a regular guest in her family's Greenwood home from 1962 onward, recruited there first by her brother Dwight or by Joyce Ladner. She recounts a visit during her time at Dillard when Carmichael and Dorie Ann Ladner came to New Orleans for Mardi Gras and talked their way past the dormitory housemother by claiming to be her siblings.

chapters.title: Closing Reflection
chapters.summary: Greene reflects on what drew her to the movement: a generation of organizers including Robert Parris Moses and James Forman who could have pursued personal advancement but instead set those possibilities aside to help others. The recurring quality she names is the absence of ambition for personal wealth or recognition.

loc_video.caption: Freddie Greene Biddle oral history interview conducted by Emilye Crosby in Washington, DC, District of Columbia, 2015 December 10
